**Lord's Cricket Ground**, better known as **Lord's**, is a [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") [venue](/wiki/List_of_Test_cricket_grounds "List of Test cricket grounds") at [St John's Wood](/wiki/St_John%27s_Wood "St John's Wood"), historically in [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex") and now in the [City of Westminster](/wiki/City_of_Westminster "City of Westminster"), [London NW8](/wiki/North_London "North London"). Named after its founder, [Thomas Lord](/wiki/Thomas_Lord "Thomas Lord"), it is owned by [Marylebone Cricket Club](/wiki/Marylebone_Cricket_Club "Marylebone Cricket Club") (MCC) and serves as the home of [Middlesex County Cricket Club](/wiki/Middlesex_County_Cricket_Club "Middlesex County Cricket Club"), the [England and Wales Cricket Board](/wiki/England_and_Wales_Cricket_Board "England and Wales Cricket Board") (ECB), [ICC Europe](/wiki/ICC_Europe "ICC Europe") and, until August 2005, the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC).

Lord's is widely referred to as the "home of cricket"[[2]](#cite_note-2) and houses the world's oldest sporting museum.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Lord's today is not on its original 18th-century site; it is the third of three [grounds](/wiki/Cricket_field "Cricket field") which Thomas Lord established between 1787 and 1814. His first ground, now referred to as [Lord's Old Ground](/wiki/Lord%27s_Old_Ground "Lord's Old Ground"), was where [Dorset Square](/wiki/Dorset_Square "Dorset Square") now stands. Lord's Middle Ground was in use from 1811 to 1813, before being abandoned for the construction of [Regent's Canal](/wiki/Regent%27s_Canal "Regent's Canal") which carved its way through the outfield. Lord's present ground is about 250 yards (230 m) north-west of the previous Middle Ground site. The ground can hold 31,100 spectators, its capacity increasing between 2017 and 2022 as part of MCC's ongoing redevelopment plans.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Dorset_square_plaque.jpg/250px-Dorset_square_plaque.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dorset_square_plaque.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground_%284644557952%29.jpg/250px-Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground_%284644557952%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground_(4644557952).jpg)

Plaques commemorating the locations of Lord's Old Ground (left) and Middle Ground (right)

Acting on behalf of members of the [White Conduit Club](/wiki/White_Conduit_Club "White Conduit Club") underwritten by [George Finch, 9th Earl of Winchilsea & Nottingham](/wiki/George_Finch,_9th_Earl_of_Winchilsea "George Finch, 9th Earl of Winchilsea")[[4]](#cite_note-4) and [Colonel the Hon. Charles Lennox](/wiki/Charles_Lennox,_4th_Duke_of_Richmond "Charles Lennox, 4th Duke of Richmond"), Thomas Lord opened his [first cricket ground](/wiki/Lord%27s_Old_Ground "Lord's Old Ground") in May 1787 on [Dorset Fields](/wiki/Dorset_Square "Dorset Square"), a site leased from the [Portman estate](/wiki/Portman_Estate "Portman Estate").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarner194617–18-5) White Conduit Club members, discontent with the ground maintenance of [White Conduit Fields](/wiki/White_Conduit_Fields "White Conduit Fields"), moved from [Islington](/wiki/Islington "Islington") to Marylebone soon afterwards reconstituting themselves as [Marylebone Cricket Club](/wiki/Marylebone_Cricket_Club "Marylebone Cricket Club") (MCC). The establishment of Lord's new ground was a welcome prospect offering more privacy for its members, with White Conduit Fields considered too far from London's fashionable [Oxford Street](/wiki/Oxford_Street "Oxford Street") and the [West End](/wiki/West_End_of_London "West End of London").[[6]](#cite_note-FIRSTS-6) The first match played at the new ground saw [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex_county_cricket_team_(pre-1864) "Middlesex county cricket team (pre-1864)") play [Essex](/wiki/Essex_county_cricket_team_(1732%E2%80%931794) "Essex county cricket team (1732–1794)").[[7]](#cite_note-WISG-7)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarner194618-8) In 1811, feeling obliged to relocate because of a rise in rent, Lord removed the turf to a nearby interim site. The Middle Ground lay on the route decreed by [parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") for the [Regent's Canal](/wiki/Regent%27s_Canal "Regent's Canal"),[[9]](#cite_note-9) in addition to its location being unpopular with patrons.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarner194618-8)[[7]](#cite_note-WISG-7)

Lord's Middle Ground's tenure on the Eyre family estate being short-lived,[[10]](#cite_note-10) Colonel Henry Samuel Eyre offered another plot nearby, where [Lord](/wiki/Thomas_Lord "Thomas Lord") was to relay his cricket turf. The new ground at [St John's Wood](/wiki/St_John%27s_Wood "St John's Wood"), leased from the Eyres, previously featured a [duck pond](/wiki/Duck_pond "Duck pond") and was situated on an incline, hence the Lord's [pitches](/wiki/Cricket_pitch "Cricket pitch") famous slope,[[11]](#cite_note-11) which at the time was recorded as sloping down 6 ft 6 in (1.98 m) from north-west to south-east, though in actuality the slope is 8 ft 1 in (2.46 m).[[12]](#cite_note-REM-12) The new ground was opened in the 1814 season, with MCC playing [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire_County_Cricket_Club "Hertfordshire County Cricket Club") in the first match at Lord's on 22 June 1814.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWarner194619-13)[[7]](#cite_note-WISG-7)

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/LordsRelocationMap.png/250px-LordsRelocationMap.png)](/wiki/File:LordsRelocationMap.png)

Progression of Lord's ground's locations

Lord's [Tavern](/wiki/Tavern "Tavern")[[14]](#cite_note-14) was built in 1813–14,[[15]](#cite_note-FORSIX-15) followed by a wooden [pavilion](/wiki/Cricket_pavilion "Cricket pavilion") in 1814.[[16]](#cite_note-16) [First-class cricket](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") was first played on the present ground in July 1814, with the MCC playing [St John's Wood Cricket Club](/wiki/St_John%27s_Wood_Cricket_Club "St John's Wood Cricket Club").[[17]](#cite_note-FCM-17) The first [century](/wiki/Century_(cricket) "Century (cricket)") to be scored at the ground in first-class cricket was made by [Frederick Woodbridge](/wiki/Frederick_Woodbridge_(cricketer) "Frederick Woodbridge (cricketer)") (107) for [Epsom](/wiki/Epsom_Cricket_Club "Epsom Cricket Club") against Middlesex, with Epsom's [Felix Ladbroke](/wiki/Felix_Ladbroke "Felix Ladbroke") (116) recording the second century in the same match.[[6]](#cite_note-FIRSTS-6) The annual [Eton v Harrow](/wiki/Eton_v_Harrow "Eton v Harrow") [match](/wiki/Game "Game"), which had first been played at Lord's Old Ground in 1805, returned to the present ground on 29 July 1818. From 1822, the fixture became almost an annual event at Lord's.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAltham196267-18)

Lord's witnessed the first double-century to be made in first-class cricket when [William Ward](/wiki/William_Ward_(cricketer,_born_1787) "William Ward (cricketer, born 1787)") scored 278 for the MCC against [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk_county_cricket_team_(1764%E2%80%931875) "Norfolk county cricket team (1764–1875)") in 1820.[[6]](#cite_note-FIRSTS-6) The original Lord's [Pavilion](/wiki/Cricket_pavilion "Cricket pavilion"), which had recently been renovated at great expense,[[6]](#cite_note-FIRSTS-6) was destroyed by fire on the night of 28 July 1825, following the first [Winchester](/wiki/Winchester_College "Winchester College") v [Harrow](/wiki/Harrow_School "Harrow School") match which had been completed that day. MCC's records and archives, including countless unique documents, were lost in the fire.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaygarth1996522-19) The Pavilion was promptly rebuilt by Lord.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In 1825, the future of the ground was placed in jeopardy when Lord proposed redeveloping the ground with housing at a time when St John's Wood was seeing rapid development. This was prevented by William Ward,[[7]](#cite_note-WISG-7) who purchased the ground from Lord for £5,000. His purchase was celebrated in the following anonymous poem:

> :   And of all who frequent the ground named after Lord,
> :   On the list first and foremost should stand Mr Ward.
> :   No man will deny, I am sure, when I say
> :   That he's without rival first bat of the day,
> :   And although he has grown a little too stout,
> :   Even Matthews is bothered at bowling him out.
> :   He's our life blood and soul in this noblest of games,
> :   And yet on our praises he's many more claims;
> :   No pride, although rich, condescending and free,
> :   And a well informed man and a City MP.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The first [University Match](/wiki/The_University_Match_(cricket) "The University Match (cricket)") between [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford_University_Cricket_Club "Oxford University Cricket Club") and [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Cricket_Club "Cambridge University Cricket Club") was held at Lord's in 1827,[[22]](#cite_note-SEVEN-22) at the instigation of [Charles Wordsworth](/wiki/Charles_Wordsworth "Charles Wordsworth"), establishing what would be the oldest first-class fixture in the world until 2020. The ground remained under the ownership of Ward until 1835, after which it was handed over to [James Dark](/wiki/James_Dark "James Dark"). The Pavilion was refurbished in 1838, with the addition of [gas lighting](/wiki/Gas_lighting "Gas lighting").[[23]](#cite_note-23) Around this time Lord's could still be considered a country ground, with open countryside to the north and west.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Lord's was described by [Lord Cottesloe](/wiki/Thomas_Fremantle,_1st_Baron_Cottesloe "Thomas Fremantle, 1st Baron Cottesloe") in 1845 as being a primitive venue, with low benches put in a circle around the ground at a good distance providing seating for spectators.[[25]](#cite_note-FTYONE-25) Improvements to the ground were gradually made, with the introduction of a telegraph scoreboard in 1846. The Pavilion was extended on its north side in 1848 with an annex providing a separate entrance to the cricket field for [professional players](/wiki/Players_cricket_team "Players cricket team"). In the same year [scorecards](/wiki/Scorecard_(cricket) "Scorecard (cricket)") were introduced for the first time, from a portable press, and drainage was installed in 1849–50.[[25]](#cite_note-FTYONE-25)

An [Australian Aboriginal cricket team](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_cricket_team_in_England_in_1868 "Australian Aboriginal cricket team in England in 1868") toured England in 1868, with Lord's hosting one of their matches to a mixed response, with *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* describing the tourists as "a travestie upon cricketing at Lord's" and "the conquered natives of a convict colony". Dark proposed to part with his interest in the ground in 1863, for the fee of £15,000 for the remaining 29+1⁄2 years of his lease. An agreement was reached in 1864, with Dark, who was seriously ill,[[26]](#cite_note-B44-26) selling his interests at Lord's for £11,000.[[22]](#cite_note-SEVEN-22)[[7]](#cite_note-WISG-7) The landlord of the ground, Isaac Moses,[[27]](#cite_note-27) offered to sell it outright for £21,000 in 1865, which was reduced to £18,150. [William Nicholson](/wiki/William_Nicholson_(distiller) "William Nicholson (distiller)"), who was a member of the MCC Committee at the time advanced the money on a mortgage, with his proposal for the MCC to buy the ground being unanimously passed at a [special general meeting](/wiki/Extraordinary_general_meeting "Extraordinary general meeting") on 2 May 1866.[[22]](#cite_note-SEVEN-22) Following this purchase, a number of developments took place. These included the addition of [cricket nets](/wiki/Cricket_nets "Cricket nets") for members to practice and the construction of a grandstand designed by the architect [Arthur Allom](/wiki/Charles_Allom "Charles Allom"), which was built in the winter of 1867–68 and also provided accommodation for [the press](/wiki/The_press "The press").[[28]](#cite_note-FTYFIVE-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-LBH-30) The stand was funded by a private syndicate of MCC members, purchased by the club in 1869.[[31]](#cite_note-GRA-31) The [wicket](/wiki/Wicket#The_pitch "Wicket") at Lord's was heavily criticised in the 1860s due to its poor condition, with [Frederick Gale](/wiki/Frederick_Gale "Frederick Gale") suggesting that nine cricket grounds out of ten within 20 miles of London would have a better wicket;[[28]](#cite_note-FTYFIVE-28) the condition was deemed so poor as to be dangerous that [Sussex](/wiki/Sussex_County_Cricket_Club "Sussex County Cricket Club") refused to play there in 1864.[[15]](#cite_note-FORSIX-15)

### Continued developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Continued developments")]

By the 1860s and 1870s, the great social occasions of the season were the public school match between Eton and Harrow, the Varsity Match between Oxford and Cambridge, and the [Gentlemen v Players](/wiki/Gentlemen_v_Players "Gentlemen v Players"), with all three matches attracting great crowds. Crowds became so large that they encroached on the playing area, which necessitated the introduction of the [boundary](/wiki/Boundary_(cricket) "Boundary (cricket)") system in 1866.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Further crowd control measures were initiated in 1871, with the introduction of [turnstiles](/wiki/Turnstile "Turnstile").[[33]](#cite_note-33) The Pavilion was expanded in the mid-1860s and shortly thereafter it was decided to replace the original tavern with a new construction commencing in December 1867.[[15]](#cite_note-FORSIX-15) At this time a nascent [county game](/wiki/County_Championship#Development_of_county_cricket "County Championship") was beginning to take shape.[[34]](#cite_note-34) With Lord's hosting more county matches, the pitches subsequently improved with [umpires](/wiki/Umpire_(cricket) "Umpire (cricket)") overseeing their preparation.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

[Middlesex County Cricket Club](/wiki/Middlesex_County_Cricket_Club "Middlesex County Cricket Club"), which had been founded in 1864, began playing their home games at Lord's in 1877 after vacating [Prince's ground in Chelsea](/wiki/Prince%27s_Cricket_Ground "Prince's Cricket Ground"),[[7]](#cite_note-WISG-7) which had been considered a serious rival to Lord's given its noble backers.[[36]](#cite_note-36) In 1873–74, an embankment was built which could accommodate 4,000 spectators in four rows of seats. Four years later a new lodge and was constructed to replace an older lodge, along with a new workshop, stables and a store room at a cost of £1,000.[[37]](#cite_note-37) To meet the ever-increasing demand of accommodating more spectators, a temporary stand was constructed on the eastern side of the ground.[[38]](#cite_note-38) After many years of complaints regarding the poor condition of the Lord's pitch, the MCC took action by installing Percy Pearce as [Ground Superintendent](/wiki/Groundskeeping "Groundskeeping") in 1874. Pearce had previously held the same position at the [County Ground, Hove](/wiki/County_Ground,_Hove "County Ground, Hove"). His appointment vastly improved the condition of the wicket, with *[The Standard](/wiki/Evening_Standard "Evening Standard")* describing them as "faultless".[[39]](#cite_note-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Lord%27s_Pavilion.jpg/250px-Lord%27s_Pavilion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord%27s_Pavilion.jpg)

Lord's Pavilion, designed by the architect [Thomas Verity](/wiki/Thomas_Verity "Thomas Verity") and built in 1889–90

The [Australian cricket team](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team") captained by [Dave Gregory](/wiki/Dave_Gregory_(cricketer) "Dave Gregory (cricketer)") first visited Lord's on 27 May 1878, defeating their MCC hosts by 9 wickets.[[40]](#cite_note-40) This was considered a shock result and established not only the fame of the Australian team, but also the ongoing rivalry between England and Australia.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Lord's hosted its first [Test match](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") during [The 1884 Ashes](/wiki/Australian_cricket_team_in_England_in_1884 "Australian cricket team in England in 1884"), becoming the third venue in England to host Test cricket after [The Oval](/wiki/The_Oval "The Oval") and [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford_Cricket_Ground "Old Trafford Cricket Ground").[[42]](#cite_note-42) The match was won by [England](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team") by an innings and 5 runs, with England's [A. G. Steel](/wiki/A._G._Steel "A. G. Steel") and [Edmund Peate](/wiki/Edmund_Peate "Edmund Peate") recording the first Test [century](/wiki/Century_(cricket) "Century (cricket)") and [five-wicket haul](/wiki/Five-wicket_haul "Five-wicket haul") at Lord's respectively.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

As part of the [Golden Jubilee Celebrations for Queen Victoria](/wiki/Golden_Jubilee_of_Queen_Victoria "Golden Jubilee of Queen Victoria") in 1887, the [Kings](/wiki/King "King") of [Belgium](/wiki/Leopold_II_of_Belgium "Leopold II of Belgium"), [Denmark](/wiki/Christian_IX_of_Denmark "Christian IX of Denmark"), [Saxony](/wiki/Albert,_King_of_Saxony "Albert, King of Saxony"), and [Portugal](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_I_of_Portugal "Luís I of Portugal") attended Lord's. It was noted that none of them had any grasp of cricket. In the same year Lord's hosted the MCC's hundredth anniversary celebrations, with MCC playing a celebratory match against [England](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team").[[44]](#cite_note-44) With only a two-tiered covered grandstand and both increasing membership and spectator numbers, it was decided to build a new pavilion at a cost of £21,000.[[30]](#cite_note-LBH-30) Construction of the new [Lord's Pavilion](/wiki/Lord%27s_Pavilion "Lord's Pavilion"), which was designed by [Thomas Verity](/wiki/Thomas_Verity "Thomas Verity"), took place in 1889–90.[[45]](#cite_note-PO15-45) The pavilion it replaced was relocated and painstakingly rebuilt on an estate in [Sussex](/wiki/Sussex "Sussex"), where it lived out its days as a glorified garden shed.[[46]](#cite_note-PAV-46) Soon after this, the MCC purchased the land to the east, known today as the Nursery Ground; this had previously been a [market garden](/wiki/Market_garden "Market garden") known as Henderson's Nursery[[47]](#cite_note-47) which grew pineapples and tulips.[[45]](#cite_note-PO15-45)[[30]](#cite_note-LBH-30)[[48]](#cite_note-48) The ground was subsequently threatened by the [Manchester, Sheffield and Lincolnshire Railway](/wiki/Manchester,_Sheffield_and_Lincolnshire_Railway "Manchester, Sheffield and Lincolnshire Railway")'s attempts to purchase land in the area for their line into [Marylebone Station](/wiki/Marylebone_Station "Marylebone Station").[[49]](#cite_note-49) After considering the company's offer, the MCC relinquished a strip of land bordering Wellington Road and was given in exchange the [Clergy Orphan School](/wiki/Clergy_Support_Trust "Clergy Support Trust") to the south.[[45]](#cite_note-PO15-45) In order to facilitate the railway into Marylebone Station, the Nursery Ground had to be dug up to allow tunnels to be built between 1894 and 1898 using the [cut-and-cover method](/wiki/Tunnel#Cut-and-cover "Tunnel"). Once completed the MS&LR Company laid a new pitch.[[50]](#cite_note-BAT-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/%22W.G.%22%2C_cricketing_reminiscences_and_personal_recollections_%281899%29_%2814784598082%29.jpg/250px-%22W.G.%22%2C_cricketing_reminiscences_and_personal_recollections_%281899%29_%2814784598082%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%22W.G.%22,_cricketing_reminiscences_and_personal_recollections_(1899)_(14784598082).jpg)

Cricket being played at Lord's in 1899

It was rumoured that subsequent [tunnelling](/wiki/Tunnel "Tunnel") under Wellington Road provided the banking for the Mound Stand, which was constructed in 1898–99 on an area previously occupied by [tennis](/wiki/Lawn_tennis "Lawn tennis") and [rackets](/wiki/Rackets_(sport) "Rackets (sport)") courts. The rapid development of Lord's was not well met by some, with critics mooting Thomas Lord would "turn in his grave" at the ground's expansion.[[45]](#cite_note-PO15-45) 1899 saw [Albert Trott](/wiki/Albert_Trott "Albert Trott") hit a [six](/wiki/Boundary_(cricket)#Six_runs "Boundary (cricket)") over the Pavilion while playing for MCC against the touring Australians, remaining as of 2026 the only batsman to do so.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) The [Imperial Cricket Conference](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") was founded by England, Australia and [South Africa](/wiki/South_African_national_cricket_team "South African national cricket team") in 1909, headquartered at Lord's.[[53]](#cite_note-PO16-53)

Lord's hosted three of the nine Test matches for the ill-fated [1912 Triangular Tournament](/wiki/1912_Triangular_Tournament "1912 Triangular Tournament") which was organised by the South African millionaire [Sir Abe Bailey](/wiki/Sir_Abe_Bailey "Sir Abe Bailey").[[54]](#cite_note-54) The ground's centenary was commemorated in June 1914 with a match between MCC, whose team was selected from the touring party from England's recent [tour of South Africa](/wiki/English_cricket_team_in_South_Africa_in_1913%E2%80%9314 "English cricket team in South Africa in 1913–14"), and a Rest of England team. The Rest of England won the three-day match by an innings and 189 runs.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Lord's was requisitioned by [the Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") during the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), accommodating the [Territorial Army](/wiki/Army_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Army Reserve (United Kingdom)") (TA), [Royal Army Medical Corps](/wiki/Royal_Army_Medical_Corps "Royal Army Medical Corps") (RAMC) and [Royal Army Service Corps](/wiki/Royal_Army_Service_Corps "Royal Army Service Corps") (RASC). Both cooking and wireless instruction classes were held at the ground for military personnel. Once the RAMC departed, the [War Office](/wiki/War_Office "War Office") used the Nursery Ground and other buildings as a training centre for [Royal Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Artillery "Royal Artillery") [cadets](/wiki/Officer_cadet "Officer cadet"). The Pavilion and its Long Room were used throughout the war for the manufacture of hay nets for horses on the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)").[[56]](#cite_note-56) Though requisitioned, Lord's held several charity cricket matches during the war, featuring military teams from the various dominions of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").[[57]](#cite_note-57) These matches were well attended and one such match in 1918 between England and the [Dominions](/wiki/Dominions_cricket_team "Dominions cricket team") was attended by [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") and the [Duke of Connaught](/wiki/Prince_Arthur,_Duke_of_Connaught_and_Strathearn "Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn").[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Inter-war years and WWII

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Inter-war years and WWII")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Lord%27s_weathervane.jpg/250px-Lord%27s_weathervane.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord%27s_weathervane.jpg)

*[Father Time](/wiki/Father_Time_(Lord%27s) "Father Time (Lord's)")* (pictured) incurred damage from an RAAF Barrage Balloon during World War Two.

First-class cricket returned to Lord's in 1919, with a series of two-day matches in the [County Championship](/wiki/1919_English_cricket_season#County_Championship "1919 English cricket season").[[60]](#cite_note-60) 1923 saw the installation of the [Grace Gates](/wiki/Grace_Gates "Grace Gates"), a tribute to [W. G. Grace](/wiki/W._G._Grace "W. G. Grace") who had died in 1915.[[61]](#cite_note-61) They were inaugurated by [Sir Stanley Jackson](/wiki/Stanley_Jackson_(cricketer) "Stanley Jackson (cricketer)"), who proposed including the words THE GREAT CRICKETER in the dedication.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMidwinter1981154-62) These gates replaced the ground's earlier, less decorative, entrance. With attendances growing in number, it was suggested that Lord's should aim to accommodate crowds of up to 40,000 for Test matches; however, its stands were considered inadequate with the Grandstand described as "hopelessly out of date".[[63]](#cite_note-63) To accommodate these crowds, the Old Grandstand was demolished and a new one was built in its place in 1926, designed by the architect [Sir Herbert Baker](/wiki/Herbert_Baker "Herbert Baker"). Completion of the stand was delayed due to the [1926 General Strike](/wiki/1926_United_Kingdom_general_strike "1926 United Kingdom general strike").[[31]](#cite_note-GRA-31) Upon its completion, Baker presented MCC with a [weather vane](/wiki/Weather_vane "Weather vane") of *[Father Time](/wiki/Father_Time_(Lord%27s) "Father Time (Lord's)")* removing the [bails](/wiki/Bail_(cricket) "Bail (cricket)") from the [stumps](/wiki/Stump_(cricket) "Stump (cricket)"), which was placed on top of the Grandstand. The full weathervane is 6 ft 6 in (1.98 m) tall, with the figure of *Father Time* standing at 5 ft 4 in (1.63 m). Baker further contributed to the landscape of Lord's by designing the Q (*now* [Allen](/wiki/Gubby_Allen "Gubby Allen")) Stand next to the Pavilion in 1934, while at the Nursery End Stands were also erected. Careful consideration was taken to preserve the [treeline](/wiki/Treeline "Treeline") dividing the main ground from the Nursery Ground.[[53]](#cite_note-PO16-53) The [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies_cricket_team "West Indies cricket team") under the captaincy of [Karl Nunes](/wiki/Karl_Nunes "Karl Nunes") played their first Test match at Lord's in 1928.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The ground later hosted the first [televised](/wiki/TV "TV") Test match during the [Second Test of The 1938 Ashes series](/wiki/Australian_cricket_team_in_England_in_1938 "Australian cricket team in England in 1938").[[30]](#cite_note-LBH-30)

The 1935 season saw the Lord's pitches badly affected by [crane fly](/wiki/Tipuloidea "Tipuloidea") larvae, known as leatherjackets. The larvae caused bald patches to appear on the pitch and had to be removed by the ground staff, although spin bowlers did gain some benefit from the bare surfaces.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

In contrast to the First World War, Lord's was only partially requisitioned by the military during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). Lord's hosted matches throughout the war for the [London Counties cricket team](/wiki/London_Counties_cricket_team "London Counties cricket team"), amongst others, which attracted large crowds. The ground was spared major damage from [the Blitz](/wiki/The_Blitz "The Blitz"). An [oil bomb](/wiki/Incendiary_device "Incendiary device") landed on the Nursery Ground in 1940, with a high-explosive bomb also narrowly missing the Nursery End Stands in December of the same year. The Grandstand and the Pavilion were hit by incendiary bombs, damaging their roofs. [AFS](/wiki/Auxiliary_Fire_Service "Auxiliary Fire Service") [firefighters](/wiki/Firefighter "Firefighter") stationed at Lord's reacted quickly and limited the damage. As the war progressed, the threat came not from the [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") but the newly developed [V-1 flying bomb](/wiki/V-1_flying_bomb "V-1 flying bomb"). Lord's had several near misses from these missiles in 1944, with one bomb landing 200 yards (180 m) short of the ground near to [Regent's Park](/wiki/Regent%27s_Park "Regent's Park").[[66]](#cite_note-WAR-66) The Nursery Ground had been requisitioned by the [Royal Auxiliary Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Auxiliary_Air_Force "Royal Auxiliary Air Force") and converted into a [barrage balloon](/wiki/Barrage_balloon "Barrage balloon") site.[[53]](#cite_note-PO16-53)[[67]](#cite_note-67) The most high-profile war damage was that to *Father Time*, when one such balloon broke loose and drifted toward the Grandstand, catching *Father Time* and depositing it into the seating at the front of the stand.[[68]](#cite_note-68) International cricket resumed at the end of the war, with Lord's hosting one of the [Victory Tests](/wiki/Victory_Tests "Victory Tests") (although not accorded Test status) between the [Australian Services cricket team](/wiki/Australian_Services_cricket_team "Australian Services cricket team") and England.[[66]](#cite_note-WAR-66)

### Post-war years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Post-war years")]

Following the end of the [war](/wiki/WWII "WWII") attendances at cricket matches grew. The gross attendance of 132,000 and the gate receipts of £43,000 at the Lord's [Second Test of The 1948 Ashes series](/wiki/Second_Test,_1948_Ashes_series "Second Test, 1948 Ashes series") was a record for a Test match in England at that time.[[69]](#cite_note-69) This demand necessitated further expansion of the ground, with the construction of the [Warner](/wiki/Pelham_Warner "Pelham Warner") Stand in 1958, which included snack bars and a press box.[[53]](#cite_note-PO16-53)[[30]](#cite_note-LBH-30) This stand was the work of the architect Kenneth Peacock and replaced an area of raised ground lined with trees from where it was traditionally possible to watch a match from the comfort of one's own [carriage](/wiki/Carriage "Carriage"). Prior to the construction of the Warner Stand, all stands at the ground were identified by letters of the alphabet.[[70]](#cite_note-WARN-70)

The record numbers of spectators who attended [Test](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") and [County Championship](/wiki/County_Championship "County Championship") matches began to decline by the end of the 1950s and cricket in England found itself from a position of 2.2 million paid County Championship spectators in 1947, dropping to 719,661 by 1963. To arrest this decline, [List A one-day cricket](/wiki/List_A_cricket "List A cricket") was introduced in 1963, with Lord's hosting its first List A match in the [1963 Gillette Cup](/wiki/1963_Gillette_Cup "1963 Gillette Cup") between Middlesex and [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire_County_Cricket_Club "Northamptonshire County Cricket Club") and later hosted the [final of the competition](/wiki/1963_Gillette_Cup_Final "1963 Gillette Cup Final") between Sussex and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire_County_Cricket_Club "Worcestershire County Cricket Club") in front of a sell-out 24,000 crowd. It was the first such final held anywhere in the world.[[71]](#cite_note-pandora-71) Lord's Tavern and its adjoining buildings were demolished in 1968 to make way for the construction of the Tavern Stand, again designed by Peacock.[[30]](#cite_note-LBH-30) The tavern was subsequently re-sited next to the Grace Gates and was complemented with a banqueting hall.[[53]](#cite_note-PO16-53) Lord's hosted its first [One Day International](/wiki/One_Day_International "One Day International") (ODI) in 1972,[[72]](#cite_note-ODI-72) with Australia defeating England by 5 wickets.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Three years later Lord's hosted the final of the [inaugural men's World Cup](/wiki/1975_Cricket_World_Cup "1975 Cricket World Cup"), with the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies_cricket_team "West Indies cricket team") triumphing over Australia.[[74]](#cite_note-74) Four years later, Lord's held the [final of the 1979 World Cup](/wiki/1979_Cricket_World_Cup_Final "1979 Cricket World Cup Final"), with the West Indies once against triumphing, this time against England.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

The first women's cricket match at Lord's took place in August 1976 when England and Australia played a 60-over ODI which [England](/wiki/England_women%27s_cricket_team "England women's cricket team") won by eight wickets. The opportunity to play a women's match at Lord's resulted from lobbying by [Rachael Heyhoe Flint](/wiki/Rachael_Heyhoe_Flint "Rachael Heyhoe Flint"), and was given extra impetus by England's victory in the [1973 Women's Cricket World Cup](/wiki/1973_Women%27s_Cricket_World_Cup "1973 Women's Cricket World Cup"). England's Women XI had to wait another 11 years to play their second match at Lord's.[[76]](#cite_note-76) The ground hosted the final of the [ICC Women's Cricket World Cup](/wiki/ICC_Women%27s_Cricket_World_Cup "ICC Women's Cricket World Cup") in [1993](/wiki/1993_Women%27s_Cricket_World_Cup_Final "1993 Women's Cricket World Cup Final") with England beating New Zealand to win the World Cup. The ground was not fully opened for the game with only 5,000 spectators attending.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground_Grand_Stand.jpg/250px-Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground_Grand_Stand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground_Grand_Stand.jpg)

Lord's Grand Stand (pictured), 1998

A new indoor cricket school was completed in 1973 at the Nursery End, launched with £75,000 from [Sir Jack Hayward](/wiki/Jack_Hayward "Jack Hayward") and additional funds raised by the [Lord's Taverners](/wiki/Lord%27s_Taverners "Lord's Taverners") and [the Sports Council](/wiki/Sport_England#The_Sports_Council_Archives "Sport England").[[78]](#cite_note-SCHOOL-78) The West Indies appeared in their [third successive World Cup final](/wiki/1983_Cricket_World_Cup_Final "1983 Cricket World Cup Final") in 1983, but were defeated by 43 runs by [India](/wiki/India_national_cricket_team "India national cricket team").[[79]](#cite_note-79) The Mound Stand's roof was removed in 1985 to make way for a new upper tier designed by [Michael Hopkins & Partners](/wiki/Hopkins_Architects "Hopkins Architects"), which opened in time for the MCC's bicentenary in 1987.[[80]](#cite_note-80) That bicentenary was celebrated with a [five-day match between MCC and a Rest of the World XI](/wiki/MCC_Bicentenary_match "MCC Bicentenary match") in August 1987, which ended in a draw after the final day was rained off.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

[Graham Gooch](/wiki/Graham_Gooch "Graham Gooch") made the first Test triple-century at Lord's, scoring 333 against India in 1990.[[82]](#cite_note-82) The final decade of the 20th–century saw rapid redevelopment of Lord's. The [Compton](/wiki/Denis_Compton "Denis Compton") and [Edrich](/wiki/Bill_Edrich "Bill Edrich") Stands were completed in 1991, having run over time and budget.[[30]](#cite_note-LBH-30) Its indoor school closed in 1994 being replaced by the new state-of-the-art Lord's Indoor Cricket Centre which opened in 1995.[[78]](#cite_note-SCHOOL-78) The old grandstand was demolished in 1996, with its replacement designed by [Nicholas Grimshaw & Partners](/wiki/Nicholas_Grimshaw "Nicholas Grimshaw") being completed in 1998. From 1997, Lord's was home to the [European Cricket Council](/wiki/European_Cricket_Council "European Cricket Council") (ECC) till 2010, and remains home to ICC Europe, which administers cricket outside of the European [full-member](/wiki/List_of_International_Cricket_Council_members "List of International Cricket Council members") nations.[[83]](#cite_note-83) With Lord's hosting three matches in the [1999 World Cup](/wiki/1999_Cricket_World_Cup "1999 Cricket World Cup"), including the [final](/wiki/1999_Cricket_World_Cup_Final "1999 Cricket World Cup Final"), the MCC set about improving press facilities by constructing the Media Centre at the Nursery End between the Compton and Edrich Stands, offering commanding views towards the Pavilion from behind the bowler's arm. Lord's Media Centre was opened in April 1999 by then MCC president [Tony Lewis](/wiki/Tony_Lewis "Tony Lewis").[[84]](#cite_note-MED-84)

### 21st-century developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 21st-century developments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/2025_Hundred_Final_at_Lord%27s.jpg/250px-2025_Hundred_Final_at_Lord%27s.jpg)](/wiki/File:2025_Hundred_Final_at_Lord%27s.jpg)

Lord's has hosted the final of [The Hundred](/wiki/The_Hundred_(cricket) "The Hundred (cricket)") since 2021.

Lord's hosted its one-hundredth Test match in June 2000, with England defeating the West Indies by two wickets; the match was also notable for the 21 wickets which fell on the second day, the most to fall in a day at a Lord's Test since 1888.[[85]](#cite_note-85) The ground also hosted the University Match over three days for the last time in 2000, after which the match alternated between [Fenner's](/wiki/Fenner%27s "Fenner's") at Cambridge and [University Parks](/wiki/University_Parks#Cricket_ground "University Parks") at Oxford.[[86]](#cite_note-86) This fixture continues at Lord's since 2001 as a one-day limited overs match.[[87]](#cite_note-87) At the start of the 21st-century, the Lord's slope which offers advantage to both [seam](/wiki/Seam_bowling "Seam bowling") and [swing bowlers](/wiki/Swing_bowling "Swing bowling") from the Pavilion and Nursery Ends respectively, was under threat of being levelled due to the advent of [drop-in pitches](/wiki/Cricket_pitch#Drop-in_pitches "Cricket pitch").[[88]](#cite_note-88) However, the MCC resisted these calls as levelling the pitch would require the rebuilding of Lord's and would mean Test cricket would not be able to be played there for five years. The [outfield](/wiki/Outfield "Outfield") was notorious for becoming waterlogged due to its clay soil, resulting in considerable lost match time. The entire outfield was relaid in the winter of 2002 with the clay soil being replaced with sand, which has improved drainage.[[89]](#cite_note-89) Lord's hosted its first [T20 match](/wiki/Twenty20 "Twenty20") in the second edition of the [Twenty20 Cup](/wiki/Twenty20_Cup "Twenty20 Cup") in 2004.[[90]](#cite_note-90) In 2005 the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (formerly the Imperial Cricket Conference) headquarters, which had been located at Lord's since its foundation in 1909,[[91]](#cite_note-91) were closed and moved to the [Dubai Sports City](/wiki/Dubai_Sports_City "Dubai Sports City") in the United Arab Emirates.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Lordsatnight.jpg/250px-Lordsatnight.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lordsatnight.jpg)

Lord's floodlit [Twenty20](/wiki/Twenty20 "Twenty20") match between Middlesex and Kent in 2009

Temporary [floodlights](/wiki/Floodlights_(sport) "Floodlights (sport)") were installed at the ground in 2007, but were removed in 2008 after complaints of [light pollution](/wiki/Light_pollution "Light pollution") from local residents. In January 2009, [Westminster City Council](/wiki/Westminster_City_Council "Westminster City Council") approved the use of new 48-metre high retractable floodlights designed to minimise light spillage into nearby residences. Council approval stipulated a five-year trial period during which up to 12 matches and 4 practice matches could be played under lights between April and September, with the lights being dimmed to half-strength at 9.50 pm and switched off by 11 pm. The floodlights were first used successfully on 27 May 2009 during the Twenty20 Cup match between Middlesex and [Kent](/wiki/Kent_County_Cricket_Club "Kent County Cricket Club").[[93]](#cite_note-93) Two weeks after the first use of the floodlights, Lord's hosted its first [Twenty20 International](/wiki/Twenty20_International "Twenty20 International") in the [World Twenty20](/wiki/2009_ICC_World_Twenty20 "2009 ICC World Twenty20") between England and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_cricket_team "Netherlands national cricket team"), which resulted in a shock last-ball win for the [ICC associate nation](/wiki/ICC_Europe "ICC Europe").[[94]](#cite_note-94) Lord's held the [2009 ICC World Twenty20 Final](/wiki/2009_ICC_World_Twenty20_Final "2009 ICC World Twenty20 Final") between [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan_national_cricket_team "Pakistan national cricket team") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka_national_cricket_team "Sri Lanka national cricket team"), which Pakistan won by 8 wickets.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

In 2008 plans were drawn up by the MCC Committee to fund the future £250 million development of the ground by constructing residential apartments and a luxury hotel along the Wellington Road and Grove End Road.[[96]](#cite_note-96)
Lord's Masterplan was unveiled in 2013, being a twenty-year plan to redevelop the ground and improve its facilities.[[97]](#cite_note-97) The first phase of its Masterplan involved the demolition and replacement of the Warner Stand with a new stand, which was built between 2015 and 2017. The new stand has improved facilities for [match officials](/wiki/Umpire_(cricket) "Umpire (cricket)") and reduced the number of restricted-view spectator seats from 600 to 100.[[98]](#cite_note-MAS-98) Phase two of Lord's Masterplan involved the demolition of the Compton and Edrich Stands in 2019, with their replacements being completed in 2021; these provided an extra 2,000 seats and for the first time were linked by a walkway bridge.[[98]](#cite_note-MAS-98)

Lord's celebrated the [two-hundredth anniversary](/wiki/Bicentenary_Celebration_match "Bicentenary Celebration match") of its present ground in 2014. To mark the occasion, an MCC XI captained by [Sachin Tendulkar](/wiki/Sachin_Tendulkar "Sachin Tendulkar") played a [Rest of the World XI](/wiki/World_XI_(cricket) "World XI (cricket)") led by [Shane Warne](/wiki/Shane_Warne "Shane Warne") in a 50-over match.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

Two matches of note were played at Lord's Cricket Ground in July 2019. The first of these was [2019 World Cup Final](/wiki/2019_Cricket_World_Cup_Final "2019 Cricket World Cup Final") between England and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_cricket_team "New Zealand national cricket team"), which ended as a tie with both sides each making 241 runs after 50 overs. The final was then decided by a [Super Over](/wiki/Super_Over "Super Over"), which also resulted in a tie. Thus, the winner was decided on the number of boundaries scored in the game and Super Over; this was England's first World Cup triumph.[[100]](#cite_note-100) A second match of note followed four days later when [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_cricket_team "Ireland cricket team") played [their first Test match at Lord's](/wiki/Irish_cricket_team_in_England_in_2019 "Irish cricket team in England in 2019"), where they bowled England out for 85 on the first morning of the match with [Tim Murtagh](/wiki/Tim_Murtagh "Tim Murtagh") taking 5 for 13. Despite this, in their second innings Ireland were dismissed for 38, the lowest Test total at Lord's and lost the match by 143 runs.[[101]](#cite_note-Lowest-101)

In August 2022 the ground's East Gate was renamed the Heyhoe Flint Gate in honour of [Baroness Heyhoe Flint](/wiki/Baroness_Heyhoe_Flint "Baroness Heyhoe Flint").[[102]](#cite_note-102)

In February 2026, it was reported that Lord's would undergo an overhaul of the Nursery End which would see the brick "prison" wall removed and a glass-fronted pavilion developed, along with two 12-storey apartment blocks to fund the redevelopment of the Tavern Stand. Potentially, a new Lord's museum, a roof over the currently open-air Compton and Edrich Stands, and transformation of the Thomas Lord building into a café and shop were also ideas considered in the plan as well.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

Many of the stands were rebuilt in the late 20th century. In 1987 the new Mound Stand, designed by [Michael Hopkins & Partners](/wiki/Hopkins_Architects "Hopkins Architects"), was opened, followed by the Grand Stand, designed by [Sir Nicholas Grimshaw](/wiki/Nicholas_Grimshaw "Nicholas Grimshaw"), in 1996.[[105]](#cite_note-105) The [Media Centre](/wiki/Lord%27s_Media_Centre "Lord's Media Centre"), opposite the Pavilion between the [Compton](/wiki/Denis_Compton "Denis Compton") and [Edrich](/wiki/Bill_Edrich "Bill Edrich") Stands, was added in 1999. Designed by [Future Systems](/wiki/Future_Systems "Future Systems"), it won the Royal Institute of British Architects' [Stirling Prize](/wiki/Stirling_Prize "Stirling Prize") for 1999.[[106]](#cite_note-106) The redevelopment of the Compton and Edrich Stands was completed in 2021, adding 2,600 seats and bringing the ground capacity to 31,100 spectators.[[107]](#cite_note-107) The two ends of Lord's pitch are the Pavilion End (south-west), for MCC members, and the Nursery End (north-east), dominated by the Media Centre.[[104]](#cite_note-Ground-104)

The current Grand Stand replaced that built in 1926 by [Sir Herbert Baker](/wiki/Sir_Herbert_Baker "Sir Herbert Baker"). Although the stand was described as "truly a thing of beauty, loved by all who gazed upon it", it did have limitations for spectators: 43% of the seats had an obstructed view of the playing area and the structure itself was becoming rotten.

### Pavilion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Pavilion")]

Main article: [Lord's Pavilion](/wiki/Lord%27s_Pavilion "Lord's Pavilion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Lord%27s_Pavilion.jpg/120px-Lord%27s_Pavilion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord%27s_Pavilion.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Lords_Long_Room.jpg/120px-Lords_Long_Room.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lords_Long_Room.jpg)

The Victorian-era Lord's Pavilion (left) and historic Long Room (right)

The current Lord's Pavilion is the ground's third and is its principal surviving Victorian edifice, having been built in 1889–90. It has been a [Grade II\* listed building](/wiki/Listed_building#Examples_of_Grade_II*_listed_buildings "Listed building") since September 1982.[[108]](#cite_note-NHLE-108) The Pavilion was constructed using brick with ornate terracotta facing, which includes terracotta gargoyles, such as "The Patriarch" which is understood to represent [Lord Harris](/wiki/George_Harris,_4th_Baron_Harris "George Harris, 4th Baron Harris").[[109]](#cite_note-109) The building consists of a long, two-storey centre section with covered seating between two end-towers which are capped with pyramidal roofs which have ornate wrought and cast iron lanterns.[[108]](#cite_note-NHLE-108) Running the full length behind the Upper Tier is the Pavilion's Roof Terrace, which affords panoramic views of the ground.[[110]](#cite_note-110) It underwent an £8 million refurbishment programme in 2004–05. The Pavilion provides inside and outdoor seating for MCC members to watch cricket including in the Long Room, the Bowlers' Bar as well as many other vantage points. Middlesex County Cricket Club and visiting counties' members can enjoy use by reciprocity of Lord's Pavilion on match days. The Pavilion also comprises the players' [dressing rooms](/wiki/Dressing_room "Dressing room") with respective viewing balconies.

The Long Room on the ground floor has been described by *Wisden's* [Lawrence Booth](/wiki/Lawrence_Booth_(cricket_writer) "Lawrence Booth (cricket writer)") as "the most evocative four walls in world cricket".[[111]](#cite_note-Booth-111) Players proceed through the Long Room on their way from the dressing room to the [cricket pitch](/wiki/Cricket_pitch "Cricket pitch"); this walk can be notoriously long and complex at Lord's. On his Test debut in 1975, [David Steele](/wiki/David_Steele_(cricketer) "David Steele (cricketer)") got lost when going out to bat "and ended up in the Pavilion's basement toilets".[[112]](#cite_note-112) Upon entering via the Long Room's rear door players take approximately 30 paces before reaching the steps leading onto the playing field.[[113]](#cite_note-113) The Long Room is adorned with paintings of famous cricket scenes and distinguished cricket administrators and players from the 18th century to the present day, featuring many English cricketers as well as those from overseas by rotation. For an overseas cricketer to have their portrait hang in the Long Room is considered a great honour, among whom are four Australians: [Sir Donald Bradman](/wiki/Sir_Donald_Bradman "Sir Donald Bradman"), [Keith Miller](/wiki/Keith_Miller "Keith Miller"), [Victor Trumper](/wiki/Victor_Trumper "Victor Trumper") and [Shane Warne](/wiki/Shane_Warne "Shane Warne").[[114]](#cite_note-114)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Lord%27s_honours_board_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Lord%27s_honours_board_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord%27s_honours_board_(cropped).jpg)

[Lord's honours boards](/wiki/Lord%27s_honours_boards "Lord's honours boards"), historically celebrating English cricketers, were replaced in 2019.

Displayed in the dressing rooms are the [Lord's honours boards](/wiki/Lord%27s_honours_boards "Lord's honours boards"), commemorating [centuries](/wiki/Century_(cricket) "Century (cricket)"), [five-wicket](/wiki/Five-wicket_haul "Five-wicket haul") and [ten-wicket hauls](/wiki/Ten-wicket_haul "Ten-wicket haul") for cricket internationals. Two honours boards for batting and bowling commemorate England players in the home dressing room,[[115]](#cite_note-115) while the batting and bowling honours boards commemorating overseas international players can be seen in the away dressing room.[[116]](#cite_note-116) Originally only these achievements in Test matches were commemorated, but in 2019 Lord's introduced an honours board for ODIs.[[117]](#cite_note-117) As of 2026 [167 players have made 240 Test centuries at Lord's](/wiki/List_of_international_cricket_centuries_at_Lord%27s#Test_centuries "List of international cricket centuries at Lord's") and [130 players have taken 186 five-wicket hauls](/wiki/List_of_international_cricket_five-wicket_hauls_at_Lord%27s#Test_match_five-wicket_hauls "List of international cricket five-wicket hauls at Lord's"). In ODI's [29 players have made 32 centuries at Lord's](/wiki/List_of_international_cricket_centuries_at_Lord%27s#One_Day_International_centuries "List of international cricket centuries at Lord's") and [14 players have taken a five-wicket haul](/wiki/List_of_international_cricket_five-wicket_hauls_at_Lord%27s#One_Day_International_five-wicket_hauls "List of international cricket five-wicket hauls at Lord's"). A separate "neutral" honours board was created in 2010 to coincide with Lord's hosting a [Test match between Australia and Pakistan](/wiki/Australian_cricket_team_against_Pakistan_in_England_in_2010 "Australian cricket team against Pakistan in England in 2010"). Australians [Warren Bardsley](/wiki/Warren_Bardsley "Warren Bardsley") and [Charlie Kelleway](/wiki/Charlie_Kelleway "Charlie Kelleway") were the first two names added to this board, commemorating their centuries against South Africa in 1912. They were joined by the Australians [Shane Watson](/wiki/Shane_Watson "Shane Watson") and [Marcus North](/wiki/Marcus_North "Marcus North"), who both took five-wicket hauls against Pakistan.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

The [dress code](/wiki/Dress_code "Dress code") in the Pavilion remains notoriously strict. Men are required to wear "ties and tailored coats and acceptable trousers with appropriate shoes" and women are required to wear "dresses; or skirts or trousers worn with blouses, and appropriate shoes".[[119]](#cite_note-119) Until 1999 ladies – with the exception of Club Patron [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") – were not permitted to enter the Pavilion during play, due to MCC's longstanding gender-based membership policy.[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121) MCC's 1998 decision to admit female members represented a historic modernisation for Lord's Pavilion and similar [clubs](/wiki/Gentlemen%27s_club "Gentlemen's club").[[122]](#cite_note-122)

### Media Centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Media Centre")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Nat_West_media_centre_cropped.jpg/250px-Nat_West_media_centre_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nat_West_media_centre_cropped.jpg)

Lord's futuristic Media Centre (pictured)

The decision to build the Media Centre was made during a meeting of the MCC Committee in 1995.[[123]](#cite_note-MEDIA-123) These plans sought to remove the previously inadequate media arrangements mostly concentrated in the Warner Stand which could accommodate 90 journalists, along with wooden shacks dotted around the ground for commentators,[[124]](#cite_note-SPORT-124) and replace them with a new purpose-built facility. It was then approved by members of the MCC at a special general meeting in December 1996.[[123]](#cite_note-MEDIA-123) A gap between the Compton and Edrich Stands was selected, with space limitations requiring the centre to stand 15 metres (49 ft) above ground level on reinforced supports from the structure around its two lift shafts. This design allowed for uninterrupted access between Lord's Main Ground and the Nursery Ground, while also allowing the movement of ground staff and their equipment.[[123]](#cite_note-MEDIA-123)

It was designed by the [Future Systems](/wiki/Future_Systems "Future Systems") architectural practice led by Czech architect [Jan Kaplický](/wiki/Jan_Kaplick%C3%BD "Jan Kaplický") and was the first all-aluminium, [semi-monocoque](/wiki/Monocoque "Monocoque") building in the world, costing about £5 million. Construction began in January 1997 and was completed in time for the 1999 World Cup. It was built in 32 sections and fitted out by Pendennis Shipyard at [Falmouth](/wiki/Falmouth,_Cornwall "Falmouth, Cornwall") in combination with [Centraalstaal](/wiki/Centraalstaal "Centraalstaal") from the Netherlands.[[125]](#cite_note-nce-125) These pieces were then delivered to Lord's where they were lowered into place during the [1998 season](/wiki/1998_English_cricket_season "1998 English cricket season").[[123]](#cite_note-MEDIA-123) The glazing on the front of the Media Centre is inclined 25° so as to eliminate reflections and glare on the pitch to minimise the visual barrier between the media and the players. The lower tier of Lord's Media Centre provides accommodation for 118 journalists, with two hospitality boxes either side which accommodate 18 people each. The top tier has radio and television commentary boxes, consisting of two television studios, two large commentary and radio commentary boxes, each holding up to six people.[[124]](#cite_note-SPORT-124) The centre's only opening window is in the broadcasting box used by BBC [Test Match Special](/wiki/Test_Match_Special "Test Match Special").[[126]](#cite_note-126) The building won eight architectural awards, including the [RIBA Stirling Prize](/wiki/RIBA_Stirling_Prize "RIBA Stirling Prize") for architecture in 1999.[[127]](#cite_note-127) The Media Centre was originally sponsored by [NatWest](/wiki/NatWest "NatWest"), with sponsorship being taken over by [Investec](/wiki/Investec "Investec") in 2007. From 2011, [J. P. Morgan](/wiki/JPMorgan_Chase "JPMorgan Chase") was sponsor,[[128]](#cite_note-128) and since 2025, [Barclays](/wiki/Barclays "Barclays") sponsors Lord's Media Centre.[[129]](#cite_note-129)

### Nursery Ground

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Nursery Ground")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Nursery_Ground%2C_Lord%27s_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3430343.jpg/250px-Nursery_Ground%2C_Lord%27s_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3430343.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nursery_Ground,_Lord%27s_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3430343.jpg)

Lord's Nursery Ground (pictured)

Purchased in two parts by the MCC in 1838 and 1887, the ground is primarily used as a practice ground and is considered to have some of the best grass [nets](/wiki/Cricket_nets "Cricket nets") in the world.[[130]](#cite_note-NUR-130) In 1895 the [Middlesex Volunteers](/wiki/Queen_Victoria%27s_Rifles "Queen Victoria's Rifles") requested the use of the Nursery Ground as a drill ground, but this was declined by the MCC.[[131]](#cite_note-BEN36-131) The Nursery Pavilion, which was constructed in 1999, overlooks the playing area of the Nursery Ground and is one of London's largest venues.[[132]](#cite_note-132) The ground has hosted one first-class cricket match in 1903, when the MCC played [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire_County_Cricket_Club "Yorkshire County Cricket Club");[[133]](#cite_note-133) the match was originally to be played on Lord's main ground, but heavy rain had fallen and in the week leading up to the match this had led to the abandonment of a match between the MCC and Nottinghamshire. The heavy rain persisted during the MCC v Yorkshire match, with the players spending the first two days of the three-day match sitting in the pavilion. However, it was deemed that the playing surface on the Nursery Ground was suitable for the third day of the match to be played there,[[134]](#cite_note-134) with both sides each batting for an innings and Yorkshire's [Wilfred Rhodes](/wiki/Wilfred_Rhodes "Wilfred Rhodes") making an [unbeaten](/wiki/Not_out "Not out") 98.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

The [Women's University Match](/wiki/The_University_Match "The University Match") was played on the Nursery Ground from 2001,[[136]](#cite_note-136) however following calls for gender equality, the 20-over fixture is played on the main Lord's ground since 2022 alongside the men's fixture.[[137]](#cite_note-137) On big match days crowds are allowed onto the outfield. The Cross Arrows Cricket Club play their home matches at the Nursery Ground towards the end of each cricket season.[[130]](#cite_note-NUR-130) The construction of the new Compton and Edrich Stands, beginning in August 2019, encroached on the Nursery Ground's playing area. In order to reclaim the playing area lost to the redevelopment of the stands, the temporary Nursery Pavilion will be demolished in 2025–26 and the playing area will be extended up to the perimeter wall running beside the Wellington Road.[[138]](#cite_note-138)

### MCC Museum and Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: MCC Museum and Library")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Ashes_Urn.jpg/250px-Ashes_Urn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ashes_Urn.jpg)

[The Ashes urn](/wiki/The_Ashes_urn "The Ashes urn") on display at the Lord's Museum

Lord's is the home of the MCC Museum, which is the oldest sports museum in the world, and contains the world's most celebrated collection of cricket [memorabilia](/wiki/Sports_memorabilia "Sports memorabilia"), including [The Ashes urn](/wiki/The_Ashes_urn "The Ashes urn").[[139]](#cite_note-MUS-139) MCC has been collecting memorabilia since 1864, its collection being originated by [Sir Spencer Ponsonby-Fane](/wiki/Spencer_Ponsonby-Fane "Spencer Ponsonby-Fane"), who subsequently became the Club Treasurer.[[140]](#cite_note-barc-140) These items were originally displayed in the Pavilion, limiting access to the collection to MCC members. Following the Second World War the collection had outgrown its home in the Pavilion, with a decision made to relocate the collection and open it to the public. The MCC moved the collection to a disused rackets court, which had fallen into disrepair during the war, with this location also acting as a memorial to the fallen members of the MCC from the two World Wars.[[141]](#cite_note-MUSLIB-141) MCC appointed [Diana Rait Kerr](/wiki/Diana_Rait_Kerr "Diana Rait Kerr"), "to whom the game owes a great debt", as the first full-time founding curator of its museum and library, a position she held from 1945 to 1968.[[140]](#cite_note-barc-140) The museum was officially opened to the public as the Imperial Memorial Collection by the [Duke of Edinburgh](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh") in 1953. During her tenure as curator, Rait Kerr secured donations of pictures, cricket equipment and other artefacts from around the world.[[141]](#cite_note-MUSLIB-141) Rait Kerr was succeeded as curator by Stephen Green in 1968.[[140]](#cite_note-barc-140) The museum today welcomes around 50,000 visitors per year.[[141]](#cite_note-MUSLIB-141)

Amongst the items on display include cricket kit used by [Victor Trumper](/wiki/Victor_Trumper "Victor Trumper"), [Jack Hobbs](/wiki/Jack_Hobbs "Jack Hobbs"), [Don Bradman](/wiki/Don_Bradman "Don Bradman"), [Shane Warne](/wiki/Shane_Warne "Shane Warne"), and others; many items related to the career of [W. G. Grace](/wiki/W._G._Grace "W. G. Grace"); and curiosities such as the [stuffed](/wiki/Taxidermy "Taxidermy") [sparrow](/wiki/Old_World_sparrow "Old World sparrow") that was 'bowled out' by [Jahangir Khan](/wiki/Jahangir_Khan_(cricketer) "Jahangir Khan (cricketer)") of Cambridge University in delivering a ball to [T. N. Pearce](/wiki/T._N._Pearce "T. N. Pearce") batting for the MCC on 3 July 1936. It also contains the battered copy of [*Wisden*](/wiki/Wisden_Cricketers%27_Almanack "Wisden Cricketers' Almanack") that helped to sustain [E. W. Swanton](/wiki/E._W._Swanton "E. W. Swanton") through his captivity in a Japanese [prisoner-of-war camp](/wiki/Prisoner-of-war_camp#Conditions_in_Japanese_camps "Prisoner-of-war camp") during the Second World War. It continues to collect historic artefacts and also commissions new paintings and photography.[[141]](#cite_note-MUSLIB-141) It contains the [Brian Johnston](/wiki/Brian_Johnston "Brian Johnston") [Memorial](/wiki/Memorial "Memorial") [Theatre](/wiki/Theatre "Theatre"), a cinema which screens historical cricket footage for visitors. The museum collaborates with a number of national museums and schools through active loans, in addition to community and tour programmes. It is a member of the [Sporting Heritage](/wiki/Sporting_Memories_Network "Sporting Memories Network") network.[[139]](#cite_note-MUS-139)

Lord's also has one of the largest and most comprehensive collections of books and publications dedicated to cricket. The MCC Library includes over 20,000 volumes and grows by around 400 volumes a year, and encourages donations from authors and publishers. It operates as a private library for MCC members on match days, but is open by appointment on non-match days.[[139]](#cite_note-MUS-139) It was expanded in the 1980s with the opening of a new library in the tennis court block to the rear of the pavilion,[[142]](#cite_note-142) having previously been housed in the Pavilion's Old Library before relocating to a small dedicated office space.[[143]](#cite_note-G436-143) In 2010, a selection of 100 duplicates from MCC's library collection was offered for auction by [Christie's](/wiki/Christie%27s "Christie's") with proceeds going to its support.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

### Gardens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Gardens")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground_Lord_Harris_Memorial.jpg/250px-Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground_Lord_Harris_Memorial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground_Lord_Harris_Memorial.jpg)

Memorial tablet (pictured) to [Lord Harris](/wiki/George_Harris,_4th_Baron_Harris "George Harris, 4th Baron Harris") in the Harris Garden

Lord's has two gardens, the Harris Garden and the Coronation Garden. The Coronation Garden was created behind the A Stand (Warner Stand) in 1952 to celebrate the [coronation of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Coronation_of_Elizabeth_II "Coronation of Elizabeth II").[[145]](#cite_note-145) It contains [weeping ash](/wiki/Fraxinus "Fraxinus") and other trees, providing a shaded area under which benches are found. Preserved in the Coronation Garden is one of the first models of mass-produced, cast iron, [heavy rollers](/wiki/Roller_(agricultural_tool)#Cricket_pitch_use "Roller (agricultural tool)") dating from the 1880s, which was in use at Lord's until 1945.[[146]](#cite_note-146) A large bronze statue of W. G. Grace stands in the Coronation Garden. The garden is popular with picnickers during major matchdays.[[147]](#cite_note-147) The Harris Garden, formerly tennis courts, was created as a [rose garden](/wiki/Rose_garden "Rose garden") in 1934 in memory of [Lord Harris](/wiki/George_Harris,_4th_Baron_Harris "George Harris, 4th Baron Harris").[[148]](#cite_note-148)[[149]](#cite_note-149) The garden was restored and re-launched in 2018. The restoration included the exposing of the flint wall which runs along the back of the garden,[[150]](#cite_note-GAR-150) and displays a memorial to Lord Harris. The flower beds in the Harris Garden were replanted in 2018 with a floral design featuring flowers from all the [Test-playing nations](/wiki/Test_cricket#Test_status "Test cricket").[[151]](#cite_note-151) The Harris Garden is available for private hire and can host up to 300 people.[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[150]](#cite_note-GAR-150)

## Other sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lord%27s&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Other sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Base-ball_in_England_-_the_match_on_Lord%27s_cricket_grounds_between_the_Red_Stockings_and_the_Athletics_-_from_a_sketch_by_Abner_Crossman._LCCN2008677252.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Base-ball_in_England_-_the_match_on_Lord%27s_cricket_grounds_between_the_Red_Stockings_and_the_Athletics_-_from_a_sketch_by_Abner_Crossman._LCCN2008677252.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Base-ball_in_England_-_the_match_on_Lord%27s_cricket_grounds_between_the_Red_Stockings_and_the_Athletics_-_from_a_sketch_by_Abner_Crossman._LCCN2008677252.tif)

Lord's 1874 [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") match between the [Boston Red Stockings](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves") and [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/Philadelphia_Athletics_(1860%E2%80%931876) "Philadelphia Athletics (1860–1876)")

[Sir Pelham Warner](/wiki/Sir_Pelham_Warner "Sir Pelham Warner") was of the opinion that the only other sport which had any proper standing at Lord's was [real tennis](/wiki/Real_tennis "Real tennis").[[153]](#cite_note-WAR292-153) A real tennis court began construction in October 1838, with the court's foundation stone being laid by [Benjamin Aislabie](/wiki/Benjamin_Aislabie "Benjamin Aislabie").[[154]](#cite_note-WAR28-154) The court was built at a cost of £4,000, which at the time was exceptionally high.[[155]](#cite_note-155) A real tennis competition was later established in 1867.[[154]](#cite_note-WAR28-154) The tennis court was demolished in 1898 to make way for the Mound Stand, with a replacement court being built behind the Pavilion in 1900 in the back garden of number 3 Grove End Road. By 2005 the MCC had a real tennis playing membership of 200.[[156]](#cite_note-156) The playing of [rackets](/wiki/Rackets_(sport) "Rackets (sport)") at Lord's dates from 1844 and is currently played in the tennis block.[[154]](#cite_note-WAR28-154) Lord's hosted the Public Schools Championship in 1866, with Harrow School triumphing. Since then the Championship has been held at [Prince's Club](/wiki/Prince%27s_Club "Prince's Club"), before moving to [Queen's Club](/wiki/Queen%27s_Club "Queen's Club").[[153]](#cite_note-WAR292-153)

With the advent of [lawn tennis](/wiki/Lawn_tennis "Lawn tennis"), a decision was made at the annual general meeting of the MCC in May 1875 to construct a tennis court, although there was strong opposition from some members.[[157]](#cite_note-157) A suggestion to standardise the rules of tennis was made at Lord's by [J. M. Heathcote](/wiki/John_Moyer_Heathcote "John Moyer Heathcote"), who was himself a pre-eminent real tennis player. On 3 March 1875 the MCC, in its capacity as the governing body for rackets and real tennis, convened a meeting at Lord's to determine the various versions of lawn tennis which existed with the aim to fully standardise the game's rules.[[158]](#cite_note-RULES-158) Amongst the various versions of lawn tennis which were demonstrated were [Major Clopton Wingfield's](/wiki/Walter_Clopton_Wingfield "Walter Clopton Wingfield") *Sphairistikè*, and [John H. Hale's](/wiki/John_Hale_(cricketer) "John Hale (cricketer)") *Germains Lawn Tennis*.[[159]](#cite_note-159) After the meeting, the MCC Tennis Committee was tasked with framing the rules. On 29 May 1875 the MCC issued the *Laws of Lawn Tennis*, the first unified rules for lawn tennis, which were adopted by the club on 24 June.[[160]](#cite_note-160)[[161]](#cite_note-161) These rules were amended by the [All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club](/wiki/All_England_Lawn_Tennis_and_Croquet_Club "All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club") for the [1877 Wimbledon Championship](/wiki/1877_Wimbledon_Championship "1877 Wimbledon Championship"), with the dimensions of the tennis courts being based on those at Lord's; the courts on which these were based are no longer used for tennis and now form part of the Harris Garden.[[162]](#cite_note-162)[[163]](#cite_note-163)

The original intention for the purchase of the northern part of the Nursery Ground in 1838 was for it to serve as an [archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery") venue.[[164]](#cite_note-164) Archery is recorded as having been played at Lord's as far back as August 1844, when visiting [Ioway Indians](/wiki/Iowa_people "Iowa people") camped at Lord's and demonstrated their archery skills.[[165]](#cite_note-165) Lord's was one of London's venues for the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"), hosting the [archery competition](/wiki/Archery_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "Archery at the 2012 Summer Olympics").[[166]](#cite_note-166) The 2012 Olympics Archery competition took place in front of the Pavilion, where the archers were positioned, with the targets placed 70 metres away just past the [square](/wiki/Cricket_pitch "Cricket pitch") and before Lord's Media Centre. Either side of the square temporary stands holding up to 5,000 spectators were erected.[[167]](#cite_note-167)

[Lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse") was first played at Lord's in 1833 by Canadian pioneers of the sport.[[168]](#cite_note-G347-168) Lacrosse returned to Lord's in 1876, when a team of Canadian Gentlemen Amateurs led by [William George Beers](/wiki/William_George_Beers "William George Beers") played an exhibition match at the ground against the [Iroquois Indians](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois").[[169]](#cite_note-169) A Canadian lacrosse team toured Great Britain again in 1883, with one exhibition match being staged at Lord's in front of several thousand spectators.[[170]](#cite_note-170) It was then played again at Lord's in October 1953 when the Kenton and Old Thorntonians Lacrosse Clubs met in a lacrosse championship match, with further fixtures following in November of the same year.[[168]](#cite_note-G347-168)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Archery_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men.jpg/250px-Archery_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men.jpg)](/wiki/File:Archery_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men.jpg)

Lord's [archery at the 2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Archery_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "Archery at the 2012 Summer Olympics")

[Baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") was first played at Lord's in 1874 when the MCC hosted a touring party of 22 baseball players from the [Boston Red Stockings](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves") and [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/Philadelphia_Athletics_(1860%E2%80%931876) "Philadelphia Athletics (1860–1876)"), being the two leading American baseball teams of the time.[[171]](#cite_note-171)[[172]](#cite_note-172) The Red Stockings defeated the Athletics 24–7 in front of a crowd of 5,000 spectators.[[173]](#cite_note-173) A baseball game was held at Lord's during the First World War to raise money for the Canadian Widows' and Orphans' Fund. A Canadian baseball team then played a team of American London residents in a match attracting 10,000 spectators.[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175)

Lord's hosted London's 1967 pre-[1968 Olympics](/wiki/1968_Summer_Olympics "1968 Summer Olympics") [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the Summer Olympics") tournament.[[176]](#cite_note-HOCK-176) One match saw [India](/wiki/India_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "India men's national field hockey team") play [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Pakistan men's national field hockey team"), broadcast live on the BBC which at the time was unprecedented for [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey").[[177]](#cite_note-177) Pakistan won the match 1–0,[[178]](#cite_note-178) while Pakistan also went on to defeat [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Belgium men's national field hockey team") later in the warm-up tournament,[[179]](#cite_note-179) before becoming Olympic Champions the following year. Lord's hosted further international hockey matches during the 1970s.[[176]](#cite_note-HOCK-176) The [University Match](/wiki/University_Match_(hockey) "University Match (hockey)") between Oxford and Cambridge Hockey Clubs took place at Lord's for twenty one years beginning in 1969.[[180]](#cite_note-180) [England](/wiki/England_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "England men's national field hockey team") beat [World Champions](/wiki/1975_Men%27s_Hockey_World_Cup "1975 Men's Hockey World Cup") India for the first time ever at this venue, in 1978.[[181]](#cite_note-181)

Other sports played at Lord's include [lawn bowls](/wiki/Lawn_bowls "Lawn bowls") and [billiards](/wiki/English_billiards "English billiards"). In 1838,[[182]](#cite_note-WAR29-182) a [bowling green](/wiki/Bowling_green "Bowling green") was constructed at the western end of the ground, and then a [billiard room](/wiki/Billiard_room "Billiard room") with two tables was added to the old Tavern,[[182]](#cite_note-WAR29-182)[[26]](#cite_note-B44-26)[[131]](#cite_note-BEN36-131) where professional billiards players competed on Mondays during the cricket season;[[153]](#cite_note-WAR292-153) In the late 1840s and early 1850s, Lord's held [Galloway pony](/wiki/Galloway_pony "Galloway pony") [races](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") after the cricket season was over, with races starting at the Tavern and finishing twenty yards south of the Pavilion.[[183]](#cite_note-183)

## Domestic records

* Three bowlers have taken a ten-wicket haul in an innings where the exact bowling figures are not recorded; however it is known they conceded less than 20 runs: they are [Edmund Hinkly](/wiki/Edmund_Hinkly "Edmund Hinkly"), [William Lillywhite](/wiki/William_Lillywhite "William Lillywhite") and [John Wisden](/wiki/John_Wisden "John Wisden"); the best bowling figures in an innings where the records are complete are by [Samuel Butler](/wiki/Samuel_Butler_(cricketer) "Samuel Butler (cricketer)") with 10/38 for Oxford University v Cambridge University in 1871[[219]](#cite_note-219)
